questions.questionText: A child with recurrent upper respiratory infections develops bilateral conductive hearing loss, dull tympanic membranes with air-fluid levels visible. Type B tympanogram. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Secretory otitis media / glue ear (Eustachian tube dysfunction → negative pressure → serous effusion in middle ear → conductive hearing loss)
questions.explanation: Core concept: SOM = middle ear effusion without acute infection → conductive hearing loss. Why correct: Most common cause of hearing loss in children. ET dysfunction → negative middle ear pressure → transudate accumulation. TM: dull, retracted, air-fluid level or air bubbles. Type B (flat) tympanogram. Why A wrong: Otosclerosis — fixation of stapes footplate, adults (20-40 years), bilateral CHL. Why C wrong: Meniere's — SNHL + vertigo + tinnitus + aural fullness (inner ear). Why D wrong: Acoustic neuroma — CN VIII schwannoma, unilateral SNHL, cerebellopontine angle mass. Clinical relevance: Management: watchful waiting → grommet/ventilation tube insertion. Address adenoid hypertrophy if present. Complications: cholesteatoma, adhesive otitis media. Exam trap: Type A tympanogram = normal. Type B = flat (effusion/perforation). Type C = negative peak (ET dysfunction). Type As = stiff (otosclerosis). Memory aid: 'Glue ear = Gluey fluid trapped in middle ear = conductive loss + flat tympanogram.' Reference: Dhingra – Middle Ear.
questions.conceptTag: SOM glue ear effusion tympanogram B grommet

questions.optionB: A sac of keratinized squamous epithelium in the middle ear that is locally destructive (erodes bone including ossicles, can cause intracranial complications)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Cholesteatoma = cyst of keratinized squamous epithelium (NOT a tumor) — locally destructive. Why correct: Misnomer — not a tumor and contains no cholesterol. Squamous epithelium migrates into middle ear (retraction pocket or perforation margin). Produces enzymes/cytokines that erode bone → ossicular destruction, fistula, facial nerve paralysis, intracranial complications. Why A wrong: Not a neoplasm (no cellular proliferation). Why C wrong: Not malignant. Why D wrong: Not vascular. Clinical relevance: Types: congenital (behind intact TM) and acquired (atticoantral CSOM — foul-smelling ear discharge, marginal/attic perforation). Treatment: ALWAYS surgical (mastoidectomy) — antibiotics alone don't cure. Exam trap: Unsafe (atticoantral) CSOM = cholesteatoma risk → needs surgery. Safe (tubotympanic) CSOM = central perforation → can be managed medically. Memory aid: 'Cholesteatoma = Can't be Cured by drugs = Cut (surgery) required.' Reference: Dhingra – CSOM.
